nodes.name: Earth Science
nodes.description: Geology, plate tectonics, atmosphere, and Earth's structure

config.prompt: Name the 4 layers of the Earth from the center to the surface

items.explanation:
Solid iron-nickel sphere at Earth's center; radius ~1,220 km
Temperature ~5,000–6,000°C — as hot as the Sun's surface, but solid due to enormous confining pressure
Rotates slightly faster than the rest of Earth (superrotation), discovered from seismic wave timing differences
Discovered in 1936 by Danish seismologist Inge Lehmann from anomalous seismic wave paths

Liquid iron-nickel layer surrounding the inner core; depth ~2,900–5,150 km below surface
Convection of this electrically conducting liquid generates Earth's magnetic field (the geodynamo)
The only liquid layer; seismic S-waves (shear waves) cannot travel through it, revealing its fluid state
Temperature ~4,000–5,000°C; the liquid state results from lower pressure than the inner core

Thickest layer, making up ~84% of Earth's volume; extends from ~30 km to ~2,900 km depth
Composed mostly of silicate rock (peridotite); solid but flows plastically over millions of years
Convection currents in the mantle are the engine that drives plate tectonics
The asthenosphere (upper mantle, ~100–700 km depth) is especially ductile — the tectonic plates slide over it
The boundary between the crust and mantle is the Mohorovičić discontinuity (Moho)

Thin outermost layer; oceanic crust is ~5–10 km thick, continental crust ~30–70 km thick
Continental crust is mostly granite (less dense); oceanic crust is mostly basalt (denser)
Oceanic crust is younger (mostly under 200 million years old); continental crust can exceed 4 billion years old
The boundary between crust and mantle is the Mohorovičić discontinuity (Moho), typically ~35 km deep under continents

items.prompt: Who proposed the theory of continental drift in 1912, arguing that today's continents were once joined as a single landmass?
items.explanation:
German meteorologist and geophysicist Alfred Wegener published *The Origin of Continents and Oceans* in 1912
Proposed that the continents were once united as the supercontinent Pangaea (~300 million years ago) and slowly drifted apart
Evidence: matching coastlines of South America and Africa; identical fossils (Glossopteris fern, Mesosaurus) on continents now separated by oceans
Rejected by most geologists in his lifetime because he could not explain the mechanism; plate tectonics (1960s) provided it
Died during a Greenland expedition in 1930, aged 50, before his theory was vindicated

items.prompt: What are the three main types of rock, formed respectively by cooling magma, accumulated sediment, and heat or pressure on existing rock?
items.explanation:
Igneous: formed from cooled magma or lava; examples include granite (intrusive) and basalt (extrusive)
Sedimentary: formed from compressed layers of sediment; examples include limestone, sandstone, shale; ~75% of the surface rock
Metamorphic: existing rock transformed by heat and/or pressure; marble (from limestone) and slate (from shale) are common examples
The rock cycle: rocks continuously transform between types through melting, cooling, erosion, burial, and heat/pressure
Fossils are found almost exclusively in sedimentary rock — other types destroy organic material through heat or pressure

items.prompt: What logarithmic scale, devised in 1935 by a seismologist at Caltech, measures the magnitude of earthquakes?
items.explanation:
Devised by Charles Richter (with Beno Gutenberg) at the California Institute of Technology in 1935
Logarithmic: each whole-number increase represents ~31.6 times more energy released (not 10×, which is a common misconception)
Originally designed for California earthquakes measured on a specific seismograph type; limited applicability globally
Largely superseded by the moment magnitude scale (Mᴇ) for large or distant earthquakes, though 'Richter scale' remains in popular use
The 1960 Valdívia, Chile earthquake (M9.5) is the most powerful ever recorded

items.prompt: What horseshoe-shaped belt encircling the Pacific Ocean accounts for about 75% of the world's volcanoes and 90% of its earthquakes?
items.explanation:
A 40,000 km arc around the Pacific basin where tectonic plates meet; includes the coasts of North and South America, Japan, the Philippines, Indonesia, and New Zealand
Formed by subduction zones where oceanic plates dive beneath continental or other oceanic plates, generating volcanic activity and earthquakes
Home to the world's deepest ocean trench (Mariana Trench, ~11 km) and many of the tallest volcanoes
The 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami (Japan) and the 1906 San Francisco earthquake both originated within the Ring of Fire
Includes the Cascadia Subduction Zone off the U.S. Pacific Northwest, which poses a major earthquake risk

items.prompt: What seismic boundary, named for a Croatian scientist who discovered it in 1909, marks the transition between Earth's crust and mantle?
items.explanation:
Discovered by Andrija Mohorovičić in 1909 by analyzing seismic wave speeds from a Croatian earthquake
Seismic waves abruptly speed up at this boundary, revealing that the material below (mantle) is denser than the crust above
Depth varies: ~5–10 km beneath ocean floors, ~30–70 km beneath continents (deepest under mountain ranges)
The 'Moho' is the target of deep-drilling projects; no borehole has ever penetrated through it — the Kola Superdeep Borehole (Russia) reached 12 km, within the crust
The composition change at the Moho: from granite/basalt (crust) to peridotite (mantle)

items.prompt: What term describes the time required for half of the radioactive atoms in a sample to undergo decay — the basis of radiometric dating?
items.explanation:
Each radioactive isotope has a fixed, characteristic half-life: after one half-life, 50% of the original atoms remain; after two, 25%; and so on
Radiometric dating uses known half-lives to calculate the age of rocks and fossils; carbon-14 (half-life ~5,730 years) dates organic material up to ~50,000 years old
For older geological samples, uranium-238 (half-life ~4.47 billion years) or potassium-40 (half-life ~1.25 billion years) are used
The concept was developed by Ernest Rutherford and Frederick Soddy in 1902
Half-life is unaffected by temperature, pressure, or chemical state — it is a fundamental nuclear property

items.prompt: What name, meaning 'all land' in Greek, is given to the supercontinent that encompassed nearly all of Earth's landmass about 300 million years ago?
items.explanation:
Name coined by Alfred Wegener from Greek pan ('all') + gaia ('earth')
Existed ~335–175 million years ago during the late Paleozoic and early Mesozoic eras
Surrounded by a single global ocean called Panthalassa; the Tethys Sea formed as Pangaea began to rift
Broke into Laurasia (northern: North America, Europe, Asia) and Gondwana (southern: South America, Africa, Antarctica, Australia, India)
Was itself assembled from earlier supercontinents; the supercontinent cycle repeats roughly every 500–600 million years

items.prompt: What process, driven by heat from Earth's core and radioactive decay in the mantle, generates the currents that move tectonic plates?
items.explanation:
Hot mantle material rises near mid-ocean ridges, spreads laterally, cools, and sinks at subduction zones — forming a convection cell
The primary mechanism driving seafloor spreading and the movement of tectonic plates (1–15 cm per year, depending on plate)
Heat sources: residual heat from Earth's formation (~4.5 billion years ago) and ongoing radioactive decay of uranium, thorium, and potassium
Mantle flow is extremely slow — viscosity is roughly 10²¹ times that of water; the mantle flows like glass over geological time
Harry Hess proposed seafloor spreading in 1960, linking mantle convection to plate tectonics

items.prompt: What temperature, equal to −273.15°C or 0 kelvin, represents the theoretical lower limit at which a system has minimum thermal energy?
items.explanation:
Defined as 0 K (kelvins) or −273.15°C; the kelvin scale is defined so that 0 K is absolute zero
The Third Law of Thermodynamics states that a system's entropy approaches a minimum as temperature approaches absolute zero, and absolute zero itself is unattainable
At absolute zero, particles would occupy their lowest quantum energy state (zero-point energy); quantum mechanics prevents complete stillness
The coldest known place in the universe is the Boomerang Nebula at ~1 K; laboratory physics has cooled atoms to within billionths of a kelvin of absolute zero
Lord Kelvin (William Thomson) formalized the concept and the absolute temperature scale in 1848

items.prompt: What apparent deflection of moving objects — caused by Earth's rotation — turns winds and ocean currents to the right in the Northern Hemisphere and to the left in the Southern?
items.explanation:
Named for French mathematician Gaspard-Gustave de Coriolis, who described it mathematically in 1835
An inertial (fictitious) force that appears in rotating reference frames; Earth's eastward rotation causes the deflection
Drives the formation of cyclones: counterclockwise in the Northern Hemisphere, clockwise in the Southern
Creates large-scale atmospheric circulation patterns (trade winds, westerlies) and the major ocean gyres
The Coriolis effect on household scales (e.g., which way a drain swirls) is negligible — far too weak to determine drain rotation, which is dominated by the basin's geometry and residual water motion
